About close ×

I am a specialist in Digital Marketing and Communications, focused on the intersection of heritage storytelling and luxury brand strategy.

biancamariaboninsegni@gmail.com

Bologna · Milan · Zurich

I was born in Bologna — growing up in the shadow of San Luca and everyday bike rides down via Saragozza. Since school, I have been drawn to how history and culture speak to us, while never losing interest in geopolitics, economics, and how these fields are interconnected.

I truly believe curiosity is the engine that should drive everything. I once spent five hours on a train from Geneva to Milan, listening to a PhD student in physics talk about spin and theoretical physics. Even if a subject feels light-years away, it is always worth sitting down and listening.

In 2018, I moved to Milan to begin my studies, graduating with a thesis titled Recontextualization of Cultural Heritage Between Aesthetics and Economics — a question that still feels urgent: what happens to heritage when we measure it only by what it produces?

My Master's led to an exchange semester in New Orleans that changed something in me. It confirmed what I already suspected: that every brand, like every city, needs a real story to last.

My professional journey spans the luxury and auction worlds — Christie's, Richemont, Campari. Through these experiences, I found that marketing is the discipline of closing the gap between where a brand is and where it could live in someone's life.

Creative DNA

Carlo Scarpa · David Hockney · Expressionism · Carlo Rovelli · Mark Twain · Han Kang · Dino Buzzati · Barbero · Rosalía · French hip-hop.

Thanks for reading — I hope you enjoy exploring the rest of the portfolio.

Experiences close ×
Campari Richemont Christie's
03/2025 — 09/2025 · Milan, Italy

Digital Media & Communications Specialist · Campari Group

At Campari Group, I served as a strategic custodian for Galleria Campari, where I focused on synchronizing the brand's historic Futurist artistic legacy with modern digital aesthetics. My role involved orchestrating a complex creative content strategy — managing the monthly editorial calendar and newsletter ecosystem to translate historical archives into high-engagement social narratives.

Beyond digital storytelling, I managed the end-to-end communication flow for corporate events and institutional delegations, ensuring absolute brand alignment across every touchpoint. My work also extended into specialized botanical research to define the aromatic identity of core ingredients for sensory branding, all while building rigorous performance analytics systems to track engagement and optimize weekly communication strategies.

01/2024 — 10/2024 · Geneva, Switzerland

Brand & Communications Coordinator · Richemont – Michelangelo Foundation

During my time with the Michelangelo Foundation, I focused on operational excellence by managing the logistical and administrative framework for Homo Faber 2024 — an initiative that required coordinating international mediation across more than 40 countries for over 50,000 visitors.

I was responsible for curating and producing creative content for the Foundation's digital platforms, specifically defining the visual and educational journey for the Young Ambassadors program. I developed impact surveys and statistical profiles to evaluate the global cultural footprint of our initiatives, and acted as a primary liaison for educational programs representing the Foundation to a global community of master artisans and institutional partners.

09/2022 — 12/2022 · Milan, Italy

Luxury Market & Client Communications · Christie's – Jewellery Department

In the Jewelry Department at Christie's, I supported the high-profile "Jewels & Watches: La Dolce Vita" auction, which achieved €1.77M in total sales. My contribution centred on coordinating professional photo shoots to create the premium digital assets and promotional materials necessary for such an event.

I conducted deep-dive market intelligence research into High-Net-Worth Individual behaviour and luxury trends to inform targeted communication strategies. Throughout the auction cycle, I was responsible for enforcing uncompromising premium standards to ensure total brand consistency across all client-facing touchpoints and digital catalogs.

Journals
New Orleans Geneva
New Orleans, 11/05/2023

"I see trees of green, red roses too, I see them bloom, for me and you. And I think to myself what a wonderful world."

That is more or less what I've been listening to all evening before heading to Louis Armstrong International Airport.

New Orleans, I'm sorry I didn't understand you right away. I feel I owe you an apology. I didn't understand you because I didn't want to. It's like someone looking at contemporary art and commenting, "I could do that too." Contemporary art is poetry; it is raw thought. It isn't the observer who needs to change, but the way of looking. It was the same with New Orleans — I was just looking with the wrong eyes.

It is a complex city. After five months, I still haven't figured it out. I've only realized that it hurts to leave. I feel pulled here; it's like a giant magnet. Only those who have visited can understand its magic. I don't want to go. New Orleans taught me that I overthink things — "Just do it!" is what its guardian trees shout at me.

"Leaving is bittersweet." I used to struggle to translate Italian idioms into English, but now it's the other way around. The teachers here are kind. I will especially miss my Modern Art and Entrepreneurship professors. I will miss the people who are a bit out of their minds but good at heart, with a certain sense of familiarity. "Go ahead baby! Thank you honey!" A little piece of my heart will stay here forever, kept by the whimsical inhabitants of the Crescent City. I hope to return soon.

New Orleans, 06/05/2023

I smell the clean scent of freshly washed hair, but I also feel the weariness in my eyes because I stayed up late last night. It's raining hard outside. It's one of those days where "Nola" decides she doesn't feel like being bright and happy anymore, and she takes a moment for herself.

It is precisely during these days that the trees of this strange city feel most "alive." I don't quite know how to describe them, but in their "tree-ness," they have something human and even magical about them. They remind me a bit of the trees in Tolkien's Silmarillion. I wouldn't be surprised if they started talking and moving on their own. In their apparent silence, you can sense this will to communicate and, at the same time, protect Uptown/Carrollton with their long, intertwined branches.

Especially during the rain, I can't help but stop and listen to them. I observe them, and I feel observed. I walk through the Tulane campus and see them for what they truly are. On these days, they stop hiding, and I see them shine with a life of their own. I see the trunks and leaves expanding and towering over the path, as if to reaffirm that they are indeed the guardians of St. Charles Avenue. Is this all just my imagination?

New Orleans, 02/24/2023

I thought about my house, the walls, the streets I saw every day. I think about the porches, the stairs of my university. I left with the idea that it would be easy, but the nostalgia I feel makes me doubt myself. It sounds like a silly thing, but I miss sleeping in my own bed so much. A package with Italian food helped fill some of that void. As soon as I opened the jar of ragù, I almost had to sit down.

I don't understand how Americans socialize. At times, it's genuinely hard to try and talk to them. Which I regret, because sometimes beautiful conversations come out of it. For example, today, while having breakfast, I talked with my roommate from Brooklyn about the Romney vs. Obama presidential elections. Aside from the fact that understanding her accent is like the Rainbow Road in Mario Kart for me, it was still interesting.

There are so many new words to learn: quirky, jaded, petty, cagey. I've started thinking in English, but I still dream in Italian. "You wear your heart on your sleeve" is what K. tells me to explain that every emotion can be read on my face. I like that metaphor. I write to remember; as I read, I jot down words I don't know. I fill page after page.

Regardless, I am a better person than when I left. I feel like I know better what is right for me.

New Orleans, 02/02/2023

It is evening, and S. is playing sweet songs on the guitar. It sounds like a lullaby. I'm only sorry I can't hear the lyrics, but that's okay. Normally, I would have been angry if someone had been playing and singing at 12:37 AM, but here, I like it. It tastes like home. A lifetime apart, different languages, different families, lives divided by an ocean. It's beautiful how music can make us all feel under the same roof, all under the same sky. Her singing lulls the four of us to sleep: J., M., K., and me. It's lovely that she does it for herself, but without intending to, she gives peace and serenity to all of us.

Geneva, August 1st, 2024

They sent me to Germany for work — to visit the A. Lange & Söhne manufacture, tucked away in the green hills of Glashütte, not far from Dresden. The town itself feels like a setting from a Brothers Grimm tale: quiet, forested, a place out of time. Just without the evil stepmothers. Nobody offered me any apples.

Inside the manufacture, it felt like the watchmaking version of Charlie and the Chocolate Factory. The engravings. The rubies placed precisely to reduce friction. The intricate choreography of energy moving through a mechanism — everything built with an almost moral sense of balance.

I asked the craftspeople what they listen to while working on those impossibly small components. "Podcasts," they said. They also mentioned their eyesight had improved since joining. I don't know why I found that so moving — perhaps because it speaks to what deep attention does to a person. They live in a quiet bubble, far from the noise of the rest of the world, and the world is better for it.

White Holes — Theoretical Astrophysics close ×
On the existence of the impossible

A white hole is the theoretical time-reverse of a black hole. Where a black hole permits matter and light to enter but never escape, a white hole expels matter and light outward — nothing can enter. It is, in the most literal sense, the universe exhaling.

The mathematics that predict black holes — Einstein's field equations — permit white holes with equal elegance. They appear as the other mouth of a wormhole: matter compressed to infinite density at a black hole's singularity might tunnel through spacetime and erupt outward elsewhere. The Big Bang itself has been proposed as a white hole event.

What fascinates most is not the object but its logic: the idea that for every impossible entry there exists, somewhere in the equations, an impossible exit. That physics keeps a kind of balance sheet. That nothing — not even singularity — is a one-way door.

Loop quantum gravity suggests that the gravitational collapse of a star does not end in a singularity but undergoes a quantum bounce, re-emerging as a white hole in a distant future — or a distant region of spacetime. The black hole's interior is the white hole's past.

"The future is open. This is the most astonishing of all the facts about the universe." — Carlo Rovelli, White Holes

Animation guide

The blue vortex is the black hole pulling matter inward. The white eruption is the white hole expelling it outward. The connecting tunnel is the wormhole.

Flatland — On Dimensions close ×
E.A. Abbott · 1884

"I call our world Flatland, not because we call it so, but to make its nature clearer to you, my happy readers, who are privileged to live in Space."

Imagine a world of only two dimensions — length and width, no height. Its inhabitants are geometric shapes: triangles, squares, hexagons. They perceive each other only as line segments, inferring form through brightness and shadow. Depth is invisible to them; it is a rumour from another world.

When a sphere passes through Flatland, the inhabitants see only a circle that appears from nowhere, grows, then shrinks and vanishes. They cannot conceive of the geometry that produced it. The sphere's attempt to explain the third dimension — "upward, not northward" — is incomprehensible. There is no word for it. No organ of perception to receive it.

The story is about the limits of perception — and how intelligence, constrained by the dimensions it inhabits, must perform a kind of imaginative leap to conceive of what lies beyond its own geometry. The square who is lifted into three dimensions weeps. Not from fear but from recognition: the whole time, there was more.

The tesseract — a four-dimensional hypercube — is to a cube what a cube is to a square. We cannot visualise it directly. We can only watch its three-dimensional shadow rotate, and feel the vertigo of a geometry our senses were not built to hold.

What might we be missing, living in our own Flatland of three dimensions?

Animation guide

Watch the sphere descend through the 2D plane — appearing as a growing circle, then vanishing. Then observe the tesseract rotating in projected 3D space.

This thing all things devours:
Birds, beasts, trees, flowers;
Gnaws iron, bites steel;
Grinds hard stones to meal;
Slays king, ruins town,
And beats mountain down.

What has it, precious? What has it?
